title: Selenium
summary: Architecture, locators, the three wait types, stale elements, Page Object Model, frames/windows/alerts, Grid, and how Selenium contrasts with Playwright — with rapid-fire Q&A.

sections.title: Architecture
sections.bodyHtml:
Selenium WebDriver drives real browsers through a browser-specific driver (chromedriver, geckodriver). Your test code sends commands over the W3C WebDriver protocol (HTTP/JSON) to the driver, which translates them into native browser actions.

Client → WebDriver protocol → browser driver → browser. Each command is a separate HTTP round trip — the root of much of the latency and flakiness you manage with waits.

Selenium 4 standardized on the W3C protocol and added relative locators and CDP access.

sections.title: Locators
sections.bodyHtml:
Eight strategies, in rough order of preference:

  • id — fastest, most stable when present.
  • name, className, tagName.
  • linkText, partialLinkText.
  • cssSelector — fast, flexible; generally preferred over XPath.
  • xpath — most powerful (can traverse up the DOM, match text) but slower and more brittle.
CSS vs XPath: prefer CSS for speed and readability; reach for XPath only when you need text matching or to walk to a parent/ancestor.

sections.title: The Three Wait Types
sections.bodyHtml:
The most important Selenium interview topic — the heart of flakiness control.

Implicit Wait

A global setting: poll the DOM up to N seconds when finding any element. Simple but blunt — applies everywhere and can mask issues.

Explicit Wait

WebDriverWait + ExpectedConditions — wait for a specific condition (element clickable, visible, present) on a specific element. The recommended approach.

Fluent Wait

An explicit wait with a custom polling interval and ignored exception types — fine-grained control.

Warning: never mix implicit and explicit waits — the combination produces unpredictable cumulative timeouts. Pick explicit. And avoid Thread.sleep() entirely.
new WebDriverWait(driver, Duration.ofSeconds(10))
    .until(ExpectedConditions.elementToBeClickable(By.id("submit")));

sections.title: StaleElementReferenceException
sections.bodyHtml:
Thrown when a previously found element reference is no longer attached to the DOM — typically after the page re-rendered or navigated. Causes and fixes:

  • Re-find the element right before interacting instead of caching it.
  • Wait for the new element after a DOM change.
  • This is exactly the problem Playwright's lazy locators avoid by re-querying automatically.

sections.title: Frames, Windows & Alerts
sections.bodyHtml:
• iframes — switchTo().frame(...) before interacting; defaultContent() to return.
  • Windows/tabs — getWindowHandles() + switchTo().window(handle).
  • Alerts — switchTo().alert() then accept()/dismiss().
  • Actions API for hover, drag-drop, key chords.

sections.title: Page Object Model & Grid
sections.bodyHtml:
POM — one class per page encapsulating locators and actions, so UI changes touch one file. PageFactory (@FindBy) is the classic Selenium helper.

Selenium Grid — distribute tests across multiple machines/browsers via a hub-and-node (Selenium 4: router/distributor/node) model for parallel, cross-browser execution. Pairs with TestNG/JUnit parallel config.

sections.title: Selenium vs Playwright
sections.bodyHtml:
AspectSelenium / Playwright
ProtocolW3C WebDriver (HTTP per command) / persistent CDP-style connection.
WaitingYou add explicit waits / auto-wait built into actions.
Network mockingLimited (CDP) / first-class.
SetupManage driver binaries / bundled browsers.
MaturityHuge ecosystem, any language/browser / newer, faster, fewer integrations.

Selenium's strengths: maturity, language breadth, real-browser fidelity, and broad grid/cloud support. Playwright's: speed, reliability, and built-in tooling.

quiz.question: Implicit vs explicit wait?
choices: Implicit polls globally for all element lookups; explicit waits for a specific condition on a specific element
Implicit waits for a condition; explicit polls globally for every lookup
Implicit pauses a fixed time; explicit polls the DOM globally
Implicit and explicit are aliases that should always be combined
quiz.correctAnswer: Implicit polls globally for all element lookups; explicit waits for a specific condition on a specific element
quiz.explanation: Implicit = global polling for all element lookups; explicit = wait for a specific condition on a specific element. Prefer explicit; never mix them.

quiz.question: Why avoid Thread.sleep?
choices: It silently ignores ExpectedConditions and never polls
It disables implicit waits globally for the rest of the session
It's a fixed blind wait — slow when too long, flaky when too short
It throws StaleElementReferenceException on slow pages
quiz.correctAnswer: It's a fixed blind wait — slow when too long, flaky when too short
quiz.explanation: It's a fixed blind wait — slow when too long, flaky when too short; explicit waits react to actual state.

quiz.question: What causes StaleElementReferenceException?
choices: A locator matched more than one element on the page
The cached element detached from the DOM after a re-render or navigation
The element was found before the implicit wait timeout elapsed
The browser driver binary version mismatched the browser version
quiz.correctAnswer: The cached element detached from the DOM after a re-render or navigation
quiz.explanation: The cached element detached from the DOM after a re-render/navigation; re-find it before interacting.

quiz.question: CSS selector vs XPath?
choices: XPath is faster and cleaner; CSS can match text and walk to ancestors
Both perform identically; the choice is purely stylistic preference
CSS is faster and cleaner; XPath can match text and traverse to parents but is slower
CSS can traverse to parents and match text; XPath only matches by id
quiz.correctAnswer: CSS is faster and cleaner; XPath can match text and traverse to parents but is slower
quiz.explanation: CSS is faster and cleaner; XPath can match text and traverse to parents/ancestors but is slower and more brittle.

quiz.question: How do you handle an iframe?
choices: Call switchTo().frame(...) before interacting, then switchTo().defaultContent() to leave
Use getWindowHandles() then switchTo().window(handle) to enter the frame
Call switchTo().alert() then accept() to access frame content
Frames are handled automatically; no context switch is ever needed
quiz.correctAnswer: Call switchTo().frame(...) before interacting, then switchTo().defaultContent() to leave
quiz.explanation: switchTo().frame(...) before interacting, then switchTo().defaultContent() to leave.

quiz.question: How does Selenium talk to the browser?
choices: It injects JavaScript directly into the page to manipulate the DOM
It opens a persistent CDP WebSocket connection to the browser
It sends W3C WebDriver commands over HTTP to a browser-specific driver
It controls the browser through OS-level screen and input automation
quiz.correctAnswer: It sends W3C WebDriver commands over HTTP to a browser-specific driver
quiz.explanation: Test code sends W3C WebDriver commands over HTTP to a browser-specific driver, which performs native actions.

quiz.question: What is Selenium Grid?
choices: A hub/node setup to run tests in parallel across multiple machines, browsers, and OSes
A built-in recorder that generates Page Object classes from clicks
A network proxy that mocks API responses during test runs
A wait strategy that polls the DOM across multiple browser tabs
quiz.correctAnswer: A hub/node setup to run tests in parallel across multiple machines, browsers, and OSes
quiz.explanation: A hub/node setup to run tests in parallel across multiple machines, browsers, and OSes.

quiz.question: How do you reduce flakiness?
choices: Add long Thread.sleep calls and combine implicit with explicit waits
Explicit waits, stable locators, re-finding elements, no sleeps, and test independence
Cache element references globally and share state between tests
Rely solely on implicit waits and XPath locators for every element
quiz.correctAnswer: Explicit waits, stable locators, re-finding elements, no sleeps, and test independence
quiz.explanation: Explicit waits, stable locators (id/css), re-finding elements, no sleeps, and test independence.
